metadata.name: NIS2 Directive
metadata.description: The Network and Information Security Directive 2 (NIS2) is a European Union directive (Directive 2022/2555) that establishes a high common level of cybersecurity across the EU. It replaces the original NIS Directive and significantly expands scope, imposes stricter requirements on essential and important entities, introduces management accountability, harmonizes incident reporting, and strengthens enforcement with substantial administrative fines.
metadata.source_organization: European Parliament and Council of the European Union
metadata.classification_reasoning: NIS2 is a binding EU directive requiring transposition into national law by October 17, 2024. It mandates cybersecurity risk management measures and incident reporting obligations for essential and important entities across critical sectors.
metadata.framework_purpose: To achieve a high common level of cybersecurity across the European Union by imposing obligations on entities operating critical infrastructure and essential services, enhancing cross-border cooperation, and harmonizing cybersecurity requirements.
metadata.framework_scope: Essential entities (energy, transport, banking, financial market infrastructures, health, drinking water, waste water, digital infrastructure, ICT service management B2B, public administration, space) and important entities (postal/courier, waste management, chemicals, food, manufacturing, digital providers, research) across all EU member states.
framework_objectives: Establish cybersecurity risk management obligations for essential and important entities
Harmonize incident reporting requirements across EU member states
Strengthen supply chain security across critical sectors
Introduce management body accountability for cybersecurity
Enhance cross-border cooperation and information sharing
Establish a proportionate supervisory and enforcement regime
metadata.target_audience: Essential and Important Entities, Management Bodies, CISOs, IT Security Teams, Compliance Officers, National Competent Authorities, CSIRTs, ENISA
metadata.penalty_for_non_compliance: Essential entities: up to EUR 10,000,000 or 2% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. Important entities: up to EUR 7,000,000 or 1.4% of total worldwide annual turnover. Management bodies may be held personally liable and face temporary bans from exercising managerial functions.
adoption_approach: Implement all Article 21 risk management measures
Establish incident detection and reporting processes per Articles 23-24
Ensure management body training and accountability
Conduct regular security audits and assessments

sections: Chapter IV: Cybersecurity Risk Management & Reporting (Articles 20-25)
Chapter VIII: Delegated and Implementing Acts (Articles 38-39)

controls.title: Risk Analysis and Information System Security Policies
controls.description: Entities shall adopt policies on risk analysis and information system security, including risk identification, assessment, and treatment processes proportionate to the risks posed.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(a) requires essential and important entities to adopt policies on risk analysis and information system security. These policies shall be based on an all-hazards approach, be proportionate to the risks faced, and take into account the state of the art, relevant European and international standards, and the cost of implementation. Entities must regularly review and update these policies to address emerging threats and changes in their operating environment.
evidence_requirements: Information security policy approved by management body
Risk treatment plan with prioritized actions

controls.title: All-Hazards Risk Assessment Approach
controls.description: Risk assessments shall follow an all-hazards approach covering cyber threats, physical threats, natural disasters, and supply chain risks to network and information systems.
controls.full_text: The risk analysis required under Article 21(2)(a) must follow an all-hazards approach that considers not only cyber threats but also physical threats, environmental hazards, insider threats, and interdependencies. The assessment must evaluate threats to the availability, integrity, confidentiality, and authenticity of data and services provided by the entity, taking account of the entity's size, exposure, and the likelihood and severity of potential security incidents.
evidence_requirements: Impact assessment covering availability, integrity, confidentiality, authenticity
Risk assessment review and update records

controls.title: Incident Handling
controls.description: Entities shall implement comprehensive incident handling procedures for prevention, detection, analysis, containment, response, and recovery from security incidents.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(b) requires essential and important entities to establish and maintain comprehensive incident handling capabilities. This includes processes for incident prevention through proactive threat monitoring, detection using appropriate tools and techniques, analysis and classification of incidents by severity, containment to limit impact, response actions to restore services, and post-incident recovery activities. Entities must define clear roles and responsibilities for incident handling, establish escalation procedures, and maintain documentation of all incident handling activities.
evidence_requirements: Incident response plan with defined roles and escalation procedures
Incident handling playbooks for common scenarios
Incident log and post-incident review records

controls.title: Incident Detection and Monitoring
controls.description: Entities shall deploy appropriate monitoring and detection capabilities to identify security incidents affecting network and information systems in a timely manner.
controls.full_text: As part of incident handling under Article 21(2)(b), entities must implement monitoring and detection capabilities commensurate with their risk profile. This includes deploying intrusion detection systems, security information and event management (SIEM) tools, log analysis, anomaly detection, and establishing a security operations capability. Detection must cover both technical indicators of compromise and operational anomalies that may indicate a security incident.

controls.title: Business Continuity and Crisis Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement business continuity measures including backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management to ensure continuity of essential services.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(c) requires entities to implement business continuity and crisis management measures. This encompasses backup management to ensure the availability and integrity of critical data, disaster recovery planning to restore services after an incident, and crisis management frameworks to coordinate organizational response during major disruptions. Entities must identify critical functions and assets, define recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs), and regularly test their business continuity and disaster recovery plans.
evidence_requirements: Business continuity plan covering critical services
Disaster recovery plan with defined RTO/RPO
Backup management policy and testing records
Crisis management framework and communication plan

controls.title: Backup Management and Data Recovery
controls.description: Entities shall implement backup strategies ensuring data availability and integrity, with regular testing of backup restoration procedures.
controls.full_text: Under the business continuity obligations of Article 21(2)(c), entities must establish comprehensive backup management including automated backup schedules, offsite and offline backup storage, backup integrity verification, and regular testing of data restoration procedures. Backup strategies must align with defined RPOs and ensure that critical data and system configurations can be recovered within established timeframes following a disruptive event.
evidence_requirements: Backup policy with schedules and retention periods

controls.title: Supply Chain Security
controls.description: Entities shall address supply chain security including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(d) mandates that entities address supply chain security by managing security risks arising from their relationships with direct suppliers and service providers. This includes assessing the security practices of suppliers, incorporating security requirements into contractual agreements, monitoring supplier compliance, and evaluating the overall security of the supply chain. Entities must take into account the vulnerabilities specific to each direct supplier and service provider, the overall quality of products and cybersecurity practices of suppliers, including their secure development procedures.
evidence_requirements: Supplier security monitoring and audit reports

controls.title: Supplier Security Assessment and Monitoring
controls.description: Entities shall assess and continuously monitor the cybersecurity practices of direct suppliers and service providers, considering product quality and development practices.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(d) requires entities to conduct thorough security assessments of their suppliers and service providers. This includes evaluating supplier cybersecurity maturity, reviewing secure development practices, assessing product and service security quality, monitoring for supply chain vulnerabilities, and establishing ongoing assurance mechanisms. Entities must consider the results of coordinated security risk assessments of critical supply chains carried out under Article 22.
evidence_requirements: Third-party security audit or certification verification

controls.title: Security in Network and Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Maintenance
controls.description: Entities shall ensure security in the acquisition, development, and maintenance of network and information systems, including vulnerability handling and disclosure.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(e) requires entities to incorporate security throughout the lifecycle of network and information systems. This covers secure procurement practices, secure software development lifecycle (SDLC) processes, security testing during development, secure configuration management, vulnerability handling procedures, and coordinated vulnerability disclosure. Entities must ensure that security requirements are defined during acquisition, security testing is performed before deployment, and vulnerabilities are identified, assessed, and remediated in a timely manner.
evidence_requirements: Secure procurement policy and security requirements in RFPs
Security testing results for systems before deployment

controls.title: Vulnerability Handling and Disclosure
controls.description: Entities shall establish procedures for identifying, assessing, and remediating vulnerabilities in their network and information systems, including coordinated vulnerability disclosure.
controls.full_text: As part of Article 21(2)(e), entities must implement vulnerability handling processes that include regular vulnerability scanning and assessment, prioritization of vulnerabilities based on risk, timely patching and remediation, and participation in coordinated vulnerability disclosure programs. Entities should establish clear timelines for vulnerability remediation based on severity, maintain an inventory of known vulnerabilities, and track remediation progress. Coordinated vulnerability disclosure policies should be published to enable responsible reporting by security researchers.
evidence_requirements: Vulnerability management policy with remediation timelines
Vulnerability scanning reports and remediation tracking
Patch management records and compliance metrics

controls.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management Effectiveness Assessment
controls.description: Entities shall establish policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk management measures.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(f) requires essential and important entities to adopt policies and procedures for assessing the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk management measures. This includes conducting regular security audits, performing penetration testing, using vulnerability assessments, and monitoring key cybersecurity performance indicators. Entities must establish a continuous improvement cycle where assessment results feed back into risk management processes, policies are updated based on findings, and management bodies are informed of the cybersecurity posture and any identified gaps.
evidence_requirements: Management body briefing records on cybersecurity effectiveness

controls.title: Basic Cyber Hygiene Practices and Cybersecurity Training
controls.description: Entities shall implement basic cyber hygiene practices and provide regular cybersecurity training to all personnel including management body members.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(g) requires entities to implement basic cyber hygiene practices and provide cybersecurity training. Cyber hygiene practices include regular software updates, secure password management, device security hardening, email security awareness, and safe internet usage practices. Training must be provided to all staff, including management body members, and should be proportionate to the entity's risk profile. Training programs must cover current threat landscape, entity-specific security policies, incident reporting procedures, and role-specific security responsibilities.
evidence_requirements: Cyber hygiene policy and practices documentation
Phishing simulation and awareness exercise results

controls.title: Management Body Cybersecurity Training
controls.description: Members of management bodies shall undergo specific cybersecurity training to enable them to identify risks, assess cybersecurity measures, and understand their impact on entity operations.
controls.full_text: Under Article 20(2) and Article 21(2)(g), members of the management bodies of essential and important entities are required to undergo training and encourage similar training for employees. This training must enable management body members to identify cybersecurity risks, evaluate cybersecurity risk management practices, assess their impact on services provided by the entity, and make informed decisions regarding cybersecurity investments and strategies. Training should cover governance responsibilities, regulatory obligations under NIS2, current threat landscape, and strategic cybersecurity risk management.
evidence_requirements: Management body training records and certifications
Training program for management on NIS2 obligations
Evidence of management body risk awareness activities

controls.title: Policies and Procedures Regarding Use of Cryptography and Encryption
controls.description: Entities shall adopt policies and procedures on the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(h) requires entities to establish and implement policies and procedures regarding the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption. This includes defining requirements for data encryption at rest and in transit, key management procedures, approved cryptographic algorithms and protocols, certificate management, and regular review of cryptographic implementations to ensure they remain effective against evolving threats. Entities must ensure that cryptographic controls are proportionate to the classification and sensitivity of the data being protected.
evidence_requirements: Key management procedures and custodian records
Encryption implementation inventory (at rest and in transit)
Cryptographic algorithm and protocol review records

controls.title: Human Resources Security and Access Control Policies
controls.description: Entities shall implement human resources security measures, access control policies, and asset management practices.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(i) requires entities to implement human resources security, access control policies, and asset management. HR security covers pre-employment screening, security responsibilities in employment contracts, security during employment including ongoing training, and procedures for employment changes and termination. Access control encompasses the principle of least privilege, role-based access, privileged access management, regular access reviews, and multi-factor authentication. Asset management includes maintaining inventories of hardware, software, and data assets.
evidence_requirements: HR security policy covering employment lifecycle
Access control policy with least privilege requirements

controls.title: Privileged Access Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement privileged access management controls including restricted administrative access, monitoring of privileged sessions, and regular review of privileged accounts.
controls.full_text: Under Article 21(2)(i), entities must implement robust privileged access management (PAM) to control and monitor access to critical systems and data. This includes identifying all privileged accounts, implementing the principle of least privilege for administrative access, deploying privileged access workstations, monitoring and recording privileged sessions, implementing just-in-time access for administrative tasks, and conducting regular reviews of privileged access rights. Emergency access procedures (break-glass) must be documented and auditable.
evidence_requirements: Privileged access management policy and procedures
Privileged account inventory and ownership records
Privileged session monitoring and audit logs
Privileged access review and recertification records

controls.title: Multi-Factor Authentication and Secure Communication
controls.description: Entities shall use multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions, and secured voice, video, and text communications, and secured emergency communication systems.
controls.full_text: Article 21(2)(j) requires entities to implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) or continuous authentication solutions where appropriate, along with secured communications. MFA must be deployed for remote access, privileged access, and access to critical systems. Secured communications include encrypted voice, video, and text messaging for sensitive communications. Emergency communication systems must be established and secured to ensure reliable communication during incidents and crises, with resilience against disruption of primary communication channels.
evidence_requirements: MFA implementation policy and deployment records
MFA coverage assessment for critical systems

controls.title: Management Body Governance and Accountability
controls.description: Management bodies of essential and important entities shall approve and oversee cybersecurity risk management measures and may be held liable for infringements.
controls.full_text: Article 20(1) establishes that the management bodies of essential and important entities shall approve the cybersecurity risk-management measures taken by those entities under Article 21, oversee their implementation, and can be held liable for infringements. This establishes direct accountability of senior leadership for cybersecurity, requiring management bodies to be actively involved in cybersecurity governance, risk acceptance decisions, resource allocation, and strategic direction. Management bodies must demonstrate due diligence in fulfilling their cybersecurity oversight responsibilities.
evidence_requirements: Board/management body approval of cybersecurity policies
Management oversight meeting minutes on cybersecurity
Documented risk acceptance decisions by management body

controls.title: Management Body Cybersecurity Competence
controls.description: Management body members shall be required to follow training and shall encourage similar training for employees on a regular basis.
controls.full_text: Article 20(2) requires management body members to follow training to gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks, assess cybersecurity risk-management practices, and evaluate their impact on the services provided by the entity. Member States shall encourage essential and important entities to offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, ensuring that all personnel have appropriate cybersecurity awareness and skills proportionate to their role and responsibilities.
evidence_requirements: Annual training plan and completion metrics

controls.title: Significant Incident Reporting Obligation
controls.description: Entities shall notify the competent authority or CSIRT of any significant incident without undue delay, following the prescribed multi-stage reporting timeline.
controls.full_text: Article 23(1) requires essential and important entities to notify the competent authority or CSIRT without undue delay of any significant incident that has a significant impact on the provision of their services. An incident is considered significant where it has caused or is capable of causing severe operational disruption of the services or financial loss for the entity, or it has affected or is capable of affecting other natural or legal persons by causing considerable material or non-material damage. Entities must establish processes to identify, classify, and report significant incidents within the prescribed timeframes.
evidence_requirements: Significant incident criteria and classification procedure
Incident notification process and workflow documentation
CSIRT/competent authority contact details and communication channels

controls.title: Early Warning Notification (24 Hours)
controls.description: Entities shall submit an early warning to the CSIRT or competent authority within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, including whether the incident is suspected of being caused by unlawful or malicious acts.
controls.full_text: Article 23(4)(a) requires that an early warning notification be submitted without undue delay and in any event within 24 hours of the entity becoming aware of the significant incident. The early warning shall indicate whether the significant incident is suspected of being caused by unlawful or malicious acts or could have a cross-border impact. This initial notification enables rapid mobilization of support and coordination resources.

controls.title: Incident Notification (72 Hours)
controls.description: Entities shall submit an incident notification within 72 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, providing an initial assessment of severity, impact, and indicators of compromise.
controls.full_text: Article 23(4)(b) requires that an incident notification be submitted without undue delay and in any event within 72 hours of the entity becoming aware of the significant incident. This notification shall update the information referred to in the early warning and provide an initial assessment of the significant incident, including its severity and impact, as well as, where available, the indicators of compromise. The incident notification provides additional detail to enable coordination and response.
evidence_requirements: Incident severity and impact assessment methodology
Indicators of compromise collection and reporting procedures

controls.title: Final Incident Report (1 Month)
controls.description: Entities shall submit a final report no later than one month after the incident notification, providing a detailed description, root cause analysis, mitigation measures, and cross-border impact.
controls.full_text: Article 23(4)(d) requires a final report to be submitted no later than one month after the submission of the incident notification under point (b). The final report shall include a detailed description of the incident, its severity, and impact; the type of threat or root cause that is likely to have triggered the incident; applied and ongoing mitigation measures; and, where applicable, the cross-border impact of the incident. This comprehensive post-incident report enables learning and systemic improvement.
evidence_requirements: Cross-border impact assessment and notification records

controls.title: Proportionate and Appropriate Measures
controls.description: When assessing which measures are appropriate, entities shall take due account of their degree of exposure to risks, their size, the likelihood and severity of incidents, and societal and economic impact.
controls.full_text: Article 21(3) stipulates that when assessing which measures referred to in Article 21(2) are appropriate, entities shall take due account of their degree of exposure to risks, their size, the likelihood of occurrence of incidents, their severity including societal and economic impact. This proportionality principle ensures that smaller entities are not burdened with the same requirements as large operators of critical infrastructure, while ensuring all entities maintain an appropriate security baseline relative to their risk profile.
evidence_requirements: Entity size and risk exposure analysis

controls.title: European and International Standards Adoption
controls.description: Entities shall consider and apply relevant European and international standards when implementing cybersecurity risk management measures.
controls.full_text: Article 21(4) states that Member States shall ensure that entities take into account the relevant European and international standards and, where applicable, relevant European cybersecurity certification schemes when implementing cybersecurity risk management measures. This promotes harmonization and interoperability of cybersecurity practices across the EU, leveraging established frameworks such as ISO 27001, ENISA guidelines, and EU cybersecurity certification schemes established under the Cybersecurity Act.
evidence_requirements: Gap analysis against relevant international standards

controls.title: Entity Registration Obligations
controls.description: Essential and important entities shall register with the competent authority providing entity name, sector, sub-sector, contact details, IP ranges, and Member State of establishment.
controls.full_text: Article 26(1) requires Member States to ensure that essential and important entities submit specific information to the competent authority, including: the name of the entity, the relevant sector and sub-sector listed in Annexes I or II, the address of main establishment and other legal establishments in the EU, up-to-date contact details including email and telephone, IP address ranges, and the Member State(s) where the entity provides services. Entities must notify any changes without undue delay and in any event within two weeks from the date of the change.
evidence_requirements: Entity registration confirmation from competent authority
IP range and service scope documentation

controls.title: National Cybersecurity Strategy
controls.description: Each Member State shall adopt a national cybersecurity strategy establishing strategic objectives, governance framework, and measures to achieve a high level of cybersecurity.
controls.full_text: Article 7(1) requires each Member State to adopt a national cybersecurity strategy providing the strategic objectives, the resources required, and appropriate policy and regulatory measures with a view to achieving and maintaining a high level of cybersecurity. The strategy must address security of supply chains, vulnerability management, internet security, and the development of cybersecurity skills and awareness. While primarily a state-level obligation, entities must align their cybersecurity programs with national strategies and participate in national cybersecurity initiatives.
evidence_requirements: Participation records in national cybersecurity initiatives

controls.title: Voluntary Cybersecurity Information Sharing
controls.description: Essential and important entities may voluntarily exchange relevant cybersecurity information including cyber threats, vulnerabilities, indicators of compromise, tactics, techniques, and procedures.
controls.full_text: Article 29(1) encourages essential and important entities to voluntarily exchange relevant cybersecurity information among themselves. This includes sharing information on cyber threats, near misses, vulnerabilities, techniques and procedures, indicators of compromise, adversarial tactics, threat actor-specific information, cybersecurity alerts, and recommendations regarding security tool configuration. Such information sharing arrangements must comply with data protection requirements and competition law, and entities should establish appropriate frameworks for trustworthy information exchange.
evidence_requirements: Information sharing agreement or arrangement documentation
Threat intelligence sharing platform participation records
Shared threat indicator and IOC records
Data protection compliance for shared information

controls.title: Network Security Architecture
controls.description: Entities shall implement appropriate network security measures including segmentation, firewalls, intrusion prevention, and secure network architecture design.
controls.full_text: Derived from Article 21(2)(e) requirements for security in network and information systems, entities must implement robust network security architecture. This includes network segmentation to isolate critical assets, deployment of next-generation firewalls and intrusion detection/prevention systems, secure network design principles, regular network security assessments, and monitoring of network traffic for anomalies. Network architecture must be documented and regularly reviewed to ensure it remains effective against evolving threats.
evidence_requirements: Network architecture diagrams and security zones
Firewall and IDS/IPS deployment and rule documentation

controls.title: Secure Configuration Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement secure configuration management for all network and information systems, including hardening standards, change management, and configuration monitoring.
controls.full_text: Under Article 21(2)(e), entities must implement secure configuration management practices for all systems within their network and information infrastructure. This includes establishing and maintaining security baselines and hardening standards for all system types, implementing formal change management processes, monitoring for configuration drift, and ensuring that only authorized changes are made to system configurations. Configuration management databases (CMDBs) should be maintained for asset tracking and configuration documentation.
evidence_requirements: Security baseline and hardening standards documentation
Change management policy and approval records
Configuration drift detection and remediation records

controls.title: Security Logging and Monitoring
controls.description: Entities shall implement comprehensive security logging and monitoring to support incident detection, investigation, and compliance assessment.
controls.full_text: Supporting both incident handling (Article 21(2)(b)) and effectiveness assessment (Article 21(2)(f)), entities must implement comprehensive security logging and monitoring. This includes capturing security-relevant events across all critical systems, centralizing log collection, implementing log integrity protections, defining log retention periods, establishing monitoring rules and alerts, and conducting regular log reviews. Logs must provide sufficient detail for incident investigation, forensic analysis, and compliance auditing purposes.
evidence_requirements: Logging policy with retention and integrity requirements
Monitoring rules and alert configuration records

controls.title: Physical and Environmental Security
controls.description: Entities shall implement physical and environmental security measures to protect network and information system infrastructure from unauthorized physical access, damage, and interference.
controls.full_text: Derived from the all-hazards approach required by Article 21(2)(a), entities must address physical and environmental security of their network and information system infrastructure. This includes physical access controls to data centers and server rooms, environmental monitoring (temperature, humidity, water, fire), power protection (UPS, generators), physical security monitoring (CCTV, intrusion detection), visitor management, and protection against natural disasters and environmental hazards. Physical security measures must be proportionate to the criticality of the assets being protected.

controls.title: Asset Management and Classification
controls.description: Entities shall maintain inventories of all network and information system assets and classify them based on criticality and sensitivity.
controls.full_text: Under Article 21(2)(i), entities must implement comprehensive asset management covering all hardware, software, data, and network assets that support their services. Assets must be inventoried with designated owners, classified based on their criticality to service delivery and sensitivity of data processed, and subject to appropriate security controls based on their classification. Asset inventories must be maintained accurately and kept up to date, with regular reconciliation to identify unauthorized or unmanaged assets.
evidence_requirements: Asset inventory with ownership and classification

controls.title: Coordinated Security Risk Assessments of Critical Supply Chains
controls.description: Entities shall participate in coordinated security risk assessments of critical supply chains as directed by the Cooperation Group, considering technical and non-technical risk factors.
controls.full_text: Article 22(1) provides that the Cooperation Group, in cooperation with the Commission and ENISA, may carry out coordinated security risk assessments of specific critical ICT services, ICT systems, or ICT products supply chains. These assessments take into account technical and non-technical risk factors, including those identified in the 5G toolbox recommendation. Essential and important entities in critical sectors shall participate in and contribute to these assessments when requested, providing information on their supply chain dependencies and vulnerabilities.
evidence_requirements: Participation records in coordinated supply chain risk assessments
Critical ICT product and service inventory
Supply chain risk assessment contributions and findings

controls.title: Notification to Service Recipients
controls.description: Where a significant incident is likely to adversely affect the provision of services, entities shall notify recipients of those services without undue delay of measures they may apply in response.
controls.full_text: Article 23(2) requires that where a significant incident is likely to adversely affect the provision of the entity's services, the entity shall notify, without undue delay, the recipients of its services that are potentially affected by that significant incident. Entities shall also inform service recipients of any measures or remedies those recipients can take in response to the threat. Entities must where appropriate also inform those recipients of the significant threat itself. Notification processes must be established in advance to ensure timely communication.
evidence_requirements: Service recipient notification procedures and templates
Communication channel documentation for service recipients
Notification records for past significant incidents
Guidance materials for service recipients on remedial actions

controls.title: European Cybersecurity Certification Schemes
controls.description: Member States may require essential and important entities to use particular ICT products, services, or processes that are certified under European cybersecurity certification schemes.
controls.full_text: Article 24(1) provides that in order to demonstrate compliance with certain requirements of Article 21, Member States may require essential and important entities to use particular ICT products, ICT services, and ICT processes, whether developed by the entity or procured from third parties, that are certified under European cybersecurity certification schemes adopted pursuant to the Cybersecurity Act (Regulation 2019/881). Entities should evaluate the availability of relevant certification schemes and consider certification as a means of demonstrating compliance.
evidence_requirements: EU cybersecurity certification scheme compliance records
Certified ICT product and service inventory

controls.title: Supervisory Regime for Essential Entities
controls.description: Essential entities shall be subject to ex ante and ex post supervisory measures including on-site inspections, security audits, security scans, and requests for evidence of cybersecurity policies.
controls.full_text: Article 31(1) establishes that Member States shall ensure competent authorities effectively supervise essential entities. Supervisory measures include on-site inspections and off-site supervision, including random checks; regular and targeted security audits performed by a qualified independent body or a competent authority; ad hoc audits based on risk assessments or available risk-related information; security scans based on objective, non-discriminatory, fair, and transparent risk assessment criteria; requests for information necessary to assess cybersecurity risk management measures; requests for evidence of implementation of cybersecurity policies.
evidence_requirements: Audit readiness documentation and evidence packages
Security audit reports from qualified independent bodies

controls.title: Supervisory Regime for Important Entities
controls.description: Important entities shall be subject to ex post supervisory measures when evidence of non-compliance is provided, with a lighter-touch regime compared to essential entities.
controls.full_text: Article 32(1) establishes that Member States shall ensure competent authorities take supervisory measures in respect of important entities when provided with evidence, indication, or information that an important entity allegedly fails to comply with NIS2, in particular Articles 21 and 23. This constitutes a lighter-touch, ex post supervisory model compared to essential entities. Measures may include on-site inspections and off-site supervision, targeted security audits, security scans, requests for information, and requests for evidence of cybersecurity policy implementation.
evidence_requirements: Corrective action plans and implementation records

controls.title: Administrative Fines for Essential Entities
controls.description: Essential entities that infringe Articles 21 or 23 shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 10,000,000 or 2% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher.
controls.full_text: Article 34(1) establishes the penalty framework for essential entities. Member States shall ensure that essential entities that infringe Article 21 or 23 are subject, in accordance with Article 34(2) and (3), to administrative fines of a maximum at least of EUR 10,000,000 or a maximum at least of 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the undertaking to which the essential entity belongs in the preceding financial year, whichever is higher. Entities must understand the potential financial impact of non-compliance and implement appropriate controls and documentation to demonstrate compliance.
evidence_requirements: Compliance gap assessment with remediation plan
Financial impact assessment of potential non-compliance
Board-level reporting on compliance status and risks

controls.title: Business Continuity Testing and Exercising
controls.description: Entities shall regularly test and exercise their business continuity and disaster recovery plans to ensure they are effective and up to date.
controls.full_text: As part of the business continuity requirements under Article 21(2)(c), entities must conduct regular testing and exercising of their business continuity and disaster recovery plans. This includes tabletop exercises, functional tests, full-scale simulations, and post-exercise reviews. Testing must validate that RTOs and RPOs can be achieved, that personnel understand their roles, that communication plans are effective, and that technical recovery procedures work as designed. Exercise results must be documented and used to improve plans and procedures.
evidence_requirements: Business continuity test and exercise schedule
Post-exercise review reports with lessons learned
Plan update records based on exercise findings

controls.title: Identity and Access Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement comprehensive identity and access management including user provisioning, authentication, authorization, access reviews, and identity lifecycle management.
controls.full_text: Under Article 21(2)(i), entities must implement comprehensive identity and access management (IAM) covering the complete identity lifecycle. This includes unique user identification, formal user provisioning and de-provisioning processes, role-based access control (RBAC), regular access reviews and recertification, session management controls, and integration with multi-factor authentication as required by Article 21(2)(j). IAM processes must ensure that only authorized individuals can access network and information systems, and that access rights are promptly adjusted when roles change or employment ends.
evidence_requirements: User provisioning and de-provisioning workflow records

controls.title: Data Protection and Privacy
controls.description: Entities shall implement data protection measures including data classification, handling procedures, data loss prevention, and alignment with GDPR requirements.
controls.full_text: Supporting the cryptography requirements of Article 21(2)(h) and the overall security obligations, entities must implement comprehensive data protection measures. This includes data classification based on sensitivity and regulatory requirements, data handling and transfer procedures, data loss prevention (DLP) controls, secure data disposal, and alignment with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) where personal data is processed. Data protection measures must ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data proportionate to its classification.

controls.title: Security Testing and Assessment
controls.description: Entities shall conduct regular security testing including vulnerability assessments, penetration testing, and red team exercises proportionate to their risk profile.
controls.full_text: As part of assessing cybersecurity risk management effectiveness under Article 21(2)(f), entities must conduct regular security testing and assessment activities. This includes automated vulnerability scanning, manual penetration testing, application security testing, red team exercises for high-risk entities, and threat-based testing. Testing scope must cover all critical systems and networks, results must be documented and tracked through remediation, and findings must be reported to management bodies as part of cybersecurity effectiveness reporting.
evidence_requirements: Penetration testing reports and remediation tracking
Vulnerability assessment results and trend analysis
Management body reporting on security testing outcomes

controls.title: Patch and Update Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement formal patch management processes ensuring timely application of security patches and updates to all network and information systems.
controls.full_text: Derived from Article 21(2)(e) requirements for maintenance of network and information systems, entities must implement formal patch management processes. This includes monitoring for available patches and security updates, risk-based prioritization of patches, testing before deployment, defined timelines for critical and non-critical patch application, tracking of patch compliance across the environment, and exception management for systems that cannot be immediately patched. Patch management must cover operating systems, applications, firmware, and network devices.
evidence_requirements: Patch management policy with defined timelines

controls.title: Third-Party and ICT Service Provider Risk Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement comprehensive third-party risk management for ICT service providers, including due diligence, contractual requirements, and ongoing monitoring.
controls.full_text: Under Article 21(2)(d) supply chain security requirements, entities must implement specific controls for managing ICT service provider risks. This includes conducting pre-engagement due diligence, incorporating cybersecurity requirements into contracts and SLAs, monitoring provider compliance with security requirements, establishing incident notification obligations in contracts, assessing concentration risks, and maintaining exit strategies. Entities must also consider the findings of coordinated supply chain assessments under Article 22 when managing third-party risks.
evidence_requirements: Security requirements in contracts and SLAs

controls.title: Incident Response Planning and Procedures
controls.description: Entities shall maintain documented incident response plans covering all phases of incident management, with defined roles, escalation paths, and communication procedures.
controls.full_text: To meet the reporting obligations under Article 23, entities must maintain comprehensive incident response plans. These plans must define incident categories and severity levels aligned with NIS2 significant incident criteria, establish clear roles and responsibilities for the incident response team, document escalation paths to management bodies and external authorities, include communication procedures for CSIRTs and competent authorities, and address coordination with law enforcement where unlawful acts are suspected. Plans must be regularly reviewed, updated, and tested to ensure effectiveness.
evidence_requirements: Incident response plan aligned with NIS2 requirements
Incident response team composition and contact details
Incident response plan testing and update records

controls.title: Change Management
controls.description: Entities shall implement formal change management processes to ensure that all changes to network and information systems are assessed for security impact before implementation.
controls.full_text: Derived from the risk management (Article 21(2)(a)) and incident prevention (Article 21(2)(b)) requirements, entities must implement formal change management processes. All changes to network and information systems, including hardware, software, configurations, and processes, must follow a defined change management workflow that includes impact and risk assessment, approval, testing, implementation, and review. Emergency change procedures must also be defined. Change management helps prevent security incidents caused by unauthorized or poorly planned changes.
evidence_requirements: Change request forms with security impact assessments
Emergency change documentation and post-implementation reviews
